On the latest episode of the Daily Mail's Deep Dive podcast, political editor James Tapsfield explains why he's unconvinced by Andy Burnham's cabinet and the economics of the new Prime Minister's 'feel good' giveaways. This week, Burnham officially became Britain's seventh Prime Minister in a decade, promising his government would be a 'circuit breaker' from the political turmoil that has gripped Westminster since the Brexit vote in 2016. The former Greater Manchester Mayor has spent his first days in charge shoring up what he claims will be a ' cost of living government', announcing a new-look Cabinet and a flurry of measures designed to save the public money. Speaking to columnist Robert Hardman, Tapsfield revealed his scepticism over several of Burnham's first moves as Prime Minister - from the 'risky' return of Louise Haigh, to doubts over whether Ed Miliband has what it takes to be Britain's Foreign Secretary.

Anorexia is a very complicated mental illness, I’m afraid the NHS is not equipped to deal with it, my Granddaughter suffered with it for over 7 years and the NHS were utterly useless, luckily her parents were able to afford Private Care and Therapy, I don’t think you ever get over it, but you can with determination and counselling understand it and control it, there are sometimes other under lying factors that are present, like Autism and ADHD, which the NHS don’t explore. Master tailor Tom Reimer claims the value of his collection at his luxury London flat was ruined due to faults with three special wine chillers installed by International Food Services Equipment Ltd. The work was carried out as part of a refurbishment of Mr Reimer's £8million Marylebone apartment in 2021. Many bottles in the collection were elite 'first growth' clarets from the Bordeaux and Rhone Valley regions, including bottles of 1982 Chateau Mouton Rothschild, which can sell for over £1,000, and 2012 Chateau Petrus Pomerol, which retails for more than £2,000. But the faulty fridges led to condensation and mould growth on the labels and some of the vintage wines 'probably' became 'corked', Mr Reimer claims, massively reducing the value of his 1,500-bottle London collection.

Orca's are highly intelligent social creatures and never attack humans unless the Orca is in captivity. And if you were suddenly dragged out of your home away from your family, locked in a tiny room, and made to jump around for your food, then you'd probably take a swing at your captor as well.

Orcas have once again proven they deserve their 'killer' reputation, as scientists capture a brutal never-before-seen behaviour. Researchers recorded the moment orcas rammed into a fish so hard that it exploded into thousands of tiny pieces. What makes this gruesome strategy even more impressive is the fact that their target was a sunfish, one of the largest and heaviest fish in the world. Sunfish can grow up to three metres long and weigh up to two tonnes, but that didn't stop a pair of orcas from popping it like a balloon.
  • 270 comments
  • 1 video

Humanoid robots have performed surgery on live animals for the very first time in an astonishing new breakthrough. As part of a pre-clinical trial, two humanoid robots were remotely operated by surgeons to perform keyhole surgery on pigs. Outstanding pictures

The awe-inspiring photos were taken as part of the David Malin Awards, which showcase the finest images taken of the night sky. The awards, named after British-Australian astronomer and astrophotographer David Frederick Malin, are run by the Central West Astronomical Society in New South Wales, Australia. Malin, now 85, is known for discovering the gigantic spiral galaxy - aptly named Malin 1 - in 1986, as well as his colour images and photographic processing techniques of astronomical objects.
  • 60 comments

A girl has told how she watched her friends die in front of her as they lay trapped upside-down in a car in a water-filled ditch before her father used Live 360 to rescue her. Adam Dudzinski, 17, and his girlfriend Chloe-Ann Naylor, 16, drowned after he lost control of a Ford Fiesta in Iwade, near Sittingbourne, Kent at around 8.30pm on January 3. But another girl, who cannot be named for legal reasons, survived after being found by her father who was using the Live 360 mobile app, which allows people to track each other's locations, Kent and Medway Coroner's Court heard.
  • 26 comments
  • share Girl's father used Live 360 to find her in upside-crashed car

  • Fabulous to see these beautiful earrings being worn again after 3000 years. This is what jewellery is for - to be displayed so we can marvel at the craftsmanship of those who made these wonderful items so those centuries ago. Claims of 'cultural appropriation' are nonsense - lots of jewellery is made from stones and metals that come from all around the world. Zendaya was not making any cultural link between the earrings and herself or the film. Who knows, Helen of Troy may have worn exactly the same jewellery? Enjoy this, appreciate it, let the jewellery live again instead of being locked in a glass cabinet in a museum.

  • "The Odyssey has already earned back its $250 million budget in its first five days in theaters with a global total of $282 million." STOP DOING THIS!!! NO - IT HAS NOT! The box office take is 50/50 with the cinema chains, so for the studio it's made $141m thus far. That's NOT $250m Moreover, the MARKETING is rumoured $125m over and ABOVE the budget. Thus the studio has SPENT $375m The movie needs to make $750m (that's $375m for the studio AND $375m to the cinema chains) to BREAK EVEN Meaning it's currently $470m SHORT of making ANY profit. You'd think after all this time the Mail would have figured out how the movie industry works but nooooooo, always with this idiotic misunderstanding.
